title_tesim: Chet Brandenburg, Oliver Hardy and Stan Laurel in The Golden Age of Comedy
caption_tesim: "The Golden Age of Comedy" Starring Will Rogers - Carole Lombard - Laurel and Hardy - Jean Harlow - Harry Langdon - Ben TurpinProduced by Robert Youngson
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: The 1958 film, "The Golden Age of Comedy," chronicles the variety of comedic styles and talent at the height of Hollywood's silent era. The film was culled from 2,000 reels of Mack Sennett and Hal Roach short film comedies and divided into eight acts with a prologue and finale. This photograph was taken from a scene from the 1928 silent film, "You're Darn Tootin'," starring Stan Laurel and Oliver Hardy.

Edgar Kennedy, an actor with over 400 film credits, also directed a couple dozen films, including the 1928 Laurel and Hardy short, "You're Darn Tootin'." He is listed with the director's credit in this film as, E. Livingston Kennedy.
The 1928 Laurel and Hardy short film, "You're Darn Tootin'," was released in Britain under its working title, "The Music Blasters."

title_tesim: Irene Lentz and Ben Turpin getting hung up in The Golden Age of Comedy
caption_tesim: "The Golden Age of Comedy" Starring Will Rogers - Carole Lombard - Laurel and Hardy - Jean Harlow - Harry Langdon - Ben TurpinProduced by Robert Youngson
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: The 1958 film, "The Golden Age of Comedy," chronicles the variety of comedic styles and talent at the height of Hollywood's silent era. The film was culled from 2,000 reels of Mack Sennett and Hal Roach short film comedies and divided into eight acts with a prologue and finale. This photograph was taken from a scene from the 1923 (released January 6, 1924) silent film, "Ten Dollars or Ten Days," starring Ben Turpin.

Irene, the prolific costume designer and wardrobe supervisor for United Artists and Columbia throughout the 1930's and 1940's, began her career as an actress in a few silent films, often credited as Irene Lentz.

title_tesim: Van Heflin, Julia Adams, and Paul Fierro in Wings Of The Hawk
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: U-I, Universal City, Calif."WINGS OF THE HAWK" --- A Pictorial SynopsisA story of Mexican Insurrectos in the regime of President Diaz and Pancho Villa, "Wings of the Hawk" was filmed in third-dimension, in Technicolor, for either wide or standard screen. Starring VAN HEFLIN and JULIA ADAMS, it introduces Abbe Lane and features George Dolenz, Antonio Moreno and Noah Beery. From a novel by Gerald Drayson Adams, screenplay is by James E. Moser. Budd Boetticher directed and Aaron Rosenberg produced for Universal-International. The story in pictures: 9 - Gallager and Carlos overpower a mine guard and plant explosives in the shaft. As Ruiz and his army arrive to take gold to Juarez, Insurrectos open fire from surrounding hills. Col. Ruiz takes the reins of the carriage carrying Raquel and her sister, Elena (Abbe Lane), but Gallager fights him. The carriage overturns, Elena is struck mortally by a bullet, Gallager knocks out Ruiz, grabs the semi-conscious Raquel and races to safety as explosions in the mine shaft engulf Ruiz and his men.9 - Gallager and Carlos overpower a mine guard and plant explosives in the shaft. title_tesim: Joan Fontaine, James Stewart and Willard Parker in a scene from You Gotta Stay Happy
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: Universal-International, Universal City, Calif. "You Gotta Stay Happy" -- A Pictorial Synopsis. Bringing Joan Fontaine and James Stewart together for the first time, "You Gotta Stay Happy" is the second picture made by Rampart Productions for Universal-International. Rampart is owned by Joan and her husband, William Dozier. The picture was adapted from Robert Carson's Saturday Evening Post serial by Producer-writer Karl Tunberg and is thoroughly screwball, one of the gayest comedies of the year. It also is the first  comedy Joan has made in four years, the last being "The Affairs of Susan." The film was directed by H. C. Potter. The story in pictures: 5. While Bullets calls a cab, Payne drags drousy Dee Dee out of the hotel, sneaking her past would-be bridegroom Henry while he checks his hotel bill. Payne now believes he is rescuing a defenseless beauty from a wolf.

title_tesim: Claude Jarman in The Yearling
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: "THE YEARLING," a story of a family of Florida backwoods folk, and the love of a small boy for his pet, is also a story of the warm understanding and human bondage which exists between a father and son.  Gregory Peck is seen as Penny Baxter with young Claude Jarman, Jr., his son Jody.  In direct contrast to her glamorous screen roles of the past is Jane Wyman as Ma Baxter, hardy pioneer whose struggle against the elements almost strangles her natural instincts of love and laughter.  As important as the stars is Flag, the deer, whose growth parallels that of his young master, Jody.  Clarence Brown directed the film which is photographed in Technicolor with Sidney Franklin as producer for Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer.  The film picturization of Marjorie Kinnan Rawlings' best-seller novel also features Don Gift, Margaret Wycherly, Chill Wills, and Henry Travers.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

description_tesim: Actor Joe Dominguez has also been credited in films as, Jose Dominguez and Joe Dominquez.

Several cast members appear in this photograph, taken from a scene in the film, "Golden Girl," (l to r):  Jimmie Dodd (seated in foreground), Dennis Day, possibly Gene Sheldon (with banjo), possibly Tex Brodus (with accordion), an unidentified 'Bandit' cast member, Joe Dominguez, Dale Robertson, Alex Montoya and Rico Alaniz.The unidentified 'Bandit' may possibly be either Charles Rivero, George Navarro or Paul Regas, all of which were also credited as 'Bandit' in the film. Actor Paul Burke was also credited as 'Bandit,' but would have been too young and bears no resemblance to the unidentified actor. Also, there are two others actors, Robert Cherry and Danny Jackson, credited in the film as 'Band Member,' besides the three that are previously mentioned and possibly identified (Jimmie Dodd, Gene Sheldon and Tex Brodus).

In color by Technicolor, "Fantasia" is re-released by Buena Vista Pictures Distribution.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Poster art for George Marshall's The Perils Of Pauline
caption_tesim: VALLEY of DOOMEPISODE 4

Text from back of photograph:REMEMBER WHEN?---Gaudy posters like this used to be displayed in front of the nickelodeons to herald another hair-raising episode in the silent flicker serial, "The Perils of Pauline," with the invincible Pearl White again facing death and destruction by every imaginable lethal device before she was saved in the nick of time for next week's installment. The early day movie lithographs picturing the peerless Pauline enduring all the horrors and tortures concocted by the black-hearted villain were reproduced for the new Paramount Technicolor feature with the original serial title. Betty Hutton plays the dauntless heroine of the cliffhanging thrillers which kept the Friday night Bijou patrons on the edge of their seats through an endless succession of breath-taking adventures. John Lund is the handsome hero and the Blonde Bombshell's co-star in the film, directed by George Marshall, which is certain to invoke many a nostalgic memory.(PLEASE CREDIT "THE PERILS OF PAULINE")

title_tesim: Edward G. Robinson and director Fritz Lang betwen scenes on Scarlet Street
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From Unviersal Studio, Universal City, Calif.;Edward G. Dresses Fit To KillYes, that's a kitchen apron - and yes, it's being put on Edward G. Robinson. That's Director Fritz Lang who's tieing the apron strings while the actor is preoccupied with the telephone. Motive bahind this odd situation is a sinister one. It's for a scene in "Scarlet Street" in which Edward G. wears the apron to play a henpecked husband who washes the dishes when his wife tells him to. But he does more than that; he murders - with an ice pick! "Scarlet Street" stars Robinson with Joan Bennett and Dan DUryea, memorable trio of "Woman in the Window" and is the creation of Diana Productions, organized by Director-producer Lang, Producer Walter Wanger, and Miss Bennett, (Mrs. Wanger). Their film will be released by Universal.

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen and Richard Todd in London after filming Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: During the filming of Happy-Go-Lovely at Elstree studios, Hollywood dancing star Vera-Ellen had no chance of looking round London. She was either rehearsing or before the cameras every day of the production. So when at last, after five months hard work, her part in the film was completed Vera-Ellen decided to see the famous landmarks of England's capital for herself. Her guide and companion for the day was Britain's top male star, Richard (Hasty Heart) Todd, who was himself sightseeing in Hollywood only a few months earlier. Together, the two made a lightning tour of the city, visiting such well-known spots as Piccadilly Circus, Trafalgar Square, Fleet Street, Green Park and the Houses of Parliament. On the steps of the famous Eros statue in Piccadilly, Richard Todd points out a landmark to Vera-Ellen.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen and David Niven in Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: In her dressing room at the Mercury Theatre, Edinburgh, Janet Jones (Vera-Ellen) talks to millionaire B.G. Bruno (David Niven). Through a misunderstanding, Janet believes Bruno to be Paul Tracy, a newspaper reporter. She has just told him the story of her life, and of how she has been given the lead in the show after being in the chorus. Bruno: Wonderful, but how did you get this break? I mean, was it simply through hard work or influence or what? Janet: What exactly do you mean by that remark?.A scene from Happy-Go-Lovely, the Associated British-Marcel Hellman Technicolor production starring David Niven, Vera-Ellen and Cesar Romero. Bruce Humberstone directs.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen and production crew members in Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Last-Minute Attention. Seated on the floor, bathed in a white spotlight is beautiful Vera-Ellen. She is about to commence her solo dance in the Finale number of Happy-Go-Lovely. But before she starts to sing the theme song Would you, Could You?, there are last-minute details to be attended to by the wardrobe and makeup departments. A light touch here and there leaves Vera-Ellen as radiant and pretty as ever - all set for her part in the colorful climax to Happy-Go-Lovely.

title_tesim: HISTORY IS MADE AT NIGHT
uniform_title_tesim: History Is Made At Night  (Motion picture)
caption_tesim: Stamped in black ink text from back of photograph: UCLAArts-SpecialCollections

Pasted-on paper with typed text from back of photograph: WW-2600-99.  Complications!--Jean Arthur, as Irene Vail in Walter Wanger's "History is Made at Night", doesn't know when she enters the fashionable New York café that the head waiter who will greet her is the romantic Parisian (Charles Boyer) with whom she's in love. Miss Arthur, shown in this scene with her jealous screen husband, Colin Clive (right), Dora Clement and Carey Harrison, is wearing a beaded style creation weighing thirty-five pounds. Frank Borzage directed this romantic comedy drama in which Boyer and Miss Arthur are co-starred.
Walter Wanger presents Charles Boyer and Jean Arthur in "History Is Made At Night" with Leo Carrillo, Colin Clive, and Ivan Lebedeff. Directed by Frank Borzage. Released through United Artists.
Handwritten in black ink text from back of photograph: 675

Pasted-on paper with typed text from back of photograph: WW-2600-109. Love In An Ice-Box--Charles Boyer and Jean Arthur don't mind the temperature as they run their own in this scene from Walter Wanger's "History is Made at Night". Boyer plays Paul Dumond, romantic maitre d'hotel of fashionable Paris and New York cafes, and Miss Arthur is Irene Vail, who convinces him a Park Avenue girl can really love a head waiter. Frank Borzage directed.
